stories.headline: How to Build a Profitable Crypto Portfolio: Risk-Adjusted Strategies
stories.summary: Crypto portfolios lose value and fail to perform primarily due to poor exposure management rather than lack of opportunities. This article explores risk-adjusted investment strategies for cryptocurrency portfolios in 2026.
stories.original_title: How to Build a Profitable Crypto Portfolio: Risk-Adjusted Strategies in 2026 — Crypto portfolios lose value and fail to perform because of an inability to manage exposure, and not from a lack of opportunities. In spite of...
stories.context: Cryptocurrency is a type of digital money that isn't controlled by any government or bank, similar to how you might have tokens or game coins. Unlike regular money kept in a savings account, cryptocurrency prices can swing wildly up and down, sometimes losing half their value in a single day. A crypto portfolio is simply a collection of different cryptocurrencies that someone owns, hoping to make money as their values change over time. Risk-adjusted strategies mean spreading money carefully across different investments so that if one crashes badly, it doesn't wipe out everything you own.
stories.why_it_matters: Many people lose money in cryptocurrency because they don't know how to protect their investments from big price swings. Learning smarter ways to build a crypto portfolio can help regular people avoid losing their life savings and possibly earn money instead of just hoping to get lucky.
stories.other_side: Some financial experts say that cryptocurrency is too risky and unstable for any strategy to fix safely. They argue that the only sure way to avoid losses in crypto is to avoid investing in it entirely, as even the smartest strategies can't protect against sudden market crashes.

stories.headline: AU Small Finance Bank Posts 22.8% Surge in Deposits Amid Growing Loan Portfolio
stories.summary: AU Small Finance Bank reported deposit growth of 22.8% year-on-year to Rs 1.53 lakh crore at the end of March, outpacing its gross loan portfolio growth of 21.3% to Rs 1.40 lakh crore. The bank's deposit base now exceeds its loan book, including securitised loans.
stories.original_title: AU Small Finance Bank logs higher deposit growth than gross loan expansion — AU Small Finance Bank reported a 22.8% year-on-year deposit growth to Rs 1.53 lakh crore at the end of March while its gross loan portfolio, which includes securitised loans, grew 21.3% year-on-year to Rs 1.40 lakh crore.
stories.context: A bank works like a giant piggy bank that takes money from people who want to save (depositors) and lends it to people who need to borrow money (loans). When you put money in a bank, they typically pay you a small percentage called interest, and when someone borrows from the bank, they pay the bank a higher interest rate - that difference is how banks make profit. Small finance banks like AU specifically focus on helping smaller businesses and regular people who might not get loans from big traditional banks. When deposits grow faster than loans, it means more people are trusting the bank with their savings than are borrowing money, which is actually considered safer for the bank because they have more cash available than they've lent out.
stories.why_it_matters: A bank having more money coming in from savers than going out in loans is a good sign that shows people trust it and it can safely lend to more people. This growth suggests India's smaller banks are helping regular people and businesses get the money they need to grow, which can create jobs and improve local economies.
stories.other_side: Some critics worry that banks growing this fast might not be checking if borrowers can actually pay the money back, leading to more bad loans in the future. Others point out that during tough economic times, people pulling their money out could leave the bank in trouble if it doesn't keep enough cash on hand.

stories.headline: US Doubling Insurance Backstop to $40B to Lure Ships Back to Hormuz
stories.summary: The Trump administration has expanded its maritime insurance guarantee program from $20 billion to $40 billion in an attempt to restart commercial shipping through the strategically critical Strait of Hormuz. Despite the increased financial backstop, shipowners continue to largely avoid the route due to ongoing security concerns.
stories.original_title: U.S. Doubles Hormuz Insurance Backstop to $40B in Hopes of Luring Ships Back — The Trump administration has doubled its maritime insurance backstop to $40 billion, expanding a flagship effort to restart commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz—even as shipowners continue to largely stay away.
stories.context: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow waterway (only about 21 miles wide at its narrowest point) between Iran and the Arabian Peninsula, and it's like a narrow hallway through which about 20% of all the world's oil must pass to reach buyers. Shipowners are companies that own cargo ships and transport goods across oceans, and they need maritime insurance - basically financial protection in case something bad happens to their ship, cargo, or crew. When there's war or conflict in an area, insurance companies either refuse to cover ships passing through or charge huge extra fees, making it too expensive or risky for shipowners to use that route. The US government created an insurance backstop program, meaning they promised to step in and cover losses if ships are attacked, trying to convince shipowners it's safe enough to use the route again.
stories.why_it_matters: The United States is trying to guarantee more insurance money to protect ships from attacks in a important waterway where oil and goods flow to the world. If this fails and ships keep avoiding the area, regular people everywhere could pay more for gas, food, and almost everything else that travels by ship.
stories.other_side: Critics argue that throwing money at insurance won't actually stop attacks and is just wasting taxpayers' money on a temporary fix that doesn't address the real problem. Some also say this approach just encourages more risky behavior by shipping companies that expect the government to bail them out when things go wrong.

stories.headline: US Banks Receive Major Capital Requirement Overhaul as SVB Fallout Endures
stories.summary: Federal regulators unveiled sweeping deregulation of bank capital requirements in March, freeing up billions of dollars for banks as Washington takes a generous approach to financial regulation. The overhaul occurs despite regulators quietly acknowledging that the core failure of Silicon Valley Bank never fully went away, raising questions about financial stability.
stories.original_title: US frees up billions for banks while quietly admitting SVB's core failure never went away — Washington is in a generous mood with its banks. In March, federal regulators unveiled a sweeping overhaul of capital requirements (the financial cushions that banks must hold to absorb losses in hard times), and the headlines wrote themselves: deregulation, …
stories.context: Banks need to keep some of their money set aside as a safety cushion - this is called capital requirements, and it's like having emergency savings separate from your regular spending money. Silicon Valley Bank (SVB) collapsed in 2023 because they had invested too much money in long-term bonds that lost value when interest rates rose, and when customers all tried to withdraw their money at once (called a bank run), the bank didn't have enough cash available to pay everyone. Bank capital requirements are rules set by government regulators to make sure banks keep enough money in reserve to handle emergencies and prevent them from failing. When regulators "deregulate" these requirements, they're making the rules looser and letting banks hold less emergency money, which banks love because it means they can lend and invest more money to make bigger profits - but it also makes the financial system more vulnerable to collapses.
stories.why_it_matters: The government is relaxing the rules about how much money banks must keep safe, letting them lend out billions more dollars to people and businesses. Supporters say this will help the economy grow by making loans easier to get, but it also means banks could be in danger if too many loans go bad at the same time.
stories.other_side: Critics warn that relaxing these rules could lead to another banking crisis like in 2008, when banks lost money and people lost their savings. Some argue that the government is putting bank profits ahead of protecting everyday people's money by ignoring the lessons from recent bank failures.

stories.headline: Middle East Conflict Driving Mortgage Rates Higher Across Canada
stories.summary: The ongoing conflict in Iran and resulting closure of the Strait of Hormuz are impacting Canadian homeowners as mortgage rates continue to rise despite the Bank of Canada holding its key interest rate steady. Experts warn that geopolitical disruptions in energy markets are directly affecting borrowing costs for Canadians facing renewals.
stories.original_title: How the Middle East war is already impacting mortgage rates in Canada — The ongoing war in Iran and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz are impacting Canadian pocketbooks in ways we might not expect. Experts warn Canadian homeowners facing mortgage renewals that rates are going up, despite the Bank of Canada holding its key inter…
stories.context: Most of the world's oil travels through the Strait of Hormuz, and when there's conflict or blockage there, less oil reaches global markets, making energy more expensive everywhere. Mortgage rates are the interest rates banks charge people who borrow money to buy homes, and these rates are heavily influenced by the overall cost of borrowing money in the economy, which connects to oil prices because oil affects inflation (how expensive everything gets). The Bank of Canada is Canada's central bank, similar to how the Federal Reserve works in America, and they set a key interest rate that influences all other interest rates throughout the Canadian economy. Even though Canada's central bank didn't change their main interest rate, the disruption in oil shipping is causing ripple effects that make mortgage rates rise anyway.
stories.why_it_matters: The conflict in the Middle East is making it more expensive for Canadians to buy homes or renew their mortgages, even though the Canadian government hasn't changed its main interest rate. This shows how problems in other parts of the world can hit regular Canadians right in their pocketbooks, making it harder for families to afford homes and pay their bills.
stories.other_side: Some experts say that blaming the conflict for higher mortgage rates is just a convenient excuse and that rates would have risen anyway due to inflation and market forces. Others argue that this connection is overstated and focusing on international conflicts distracts from domestic issues Canadians actually have control over.

stories.headline: Five EU Finance Ministers Demand Windfall Tax on Energy Profits
stories.summary: Five European Union finance ministers are jointly urging the EU Commission to implement a windfall profit tax on energy companies amid surging fuel prices linked to the ongoing Iran war. The proposed tax aims to capture excess profits generated by energy companies during the crisis period.
stories.original_title: Israel, Iran war: Five EU finance ministers call for windfall profit tax on energy companies — US-Israel Iran War: Five European Union finance ministers are urging the EU Commission to implement a tax on energy companies' windfall profits. This move comes in response to soaring fuel prices, which are being linked to the ongoing Iran war. The ministers …
stories.context: A windfall profit tax is a special tax that governments sometimes slap on companies when those companies suddenly make way more money than usual, usually because of some crisis or unexpected event. When war or other problems disrupt oil supplies, energy companies can sell their oil and gas at much higher prices, leading to enormous profits that didn't come from being smarter or working harder - they came from lucky circumstances that hurt regular people through higher gas and heating bills. European Union finance ministers are the top money officials from each EU country, and when multiple ministers jointly demand something, they're trying to pressure the EU Commission (which makes EU-wide rules) to take action. The idea behind the tax is that since energy companies are benefiting from a crisis that is hurting everyone else, they should give some of that extra money back to help the people who are suffering from high energy prices.
stories.why_it_matters: Some European countries want to take away extra money that energy companies are making during the crisis and use it to help regular people facing high fuel bills. This could lower the cost of heating and driving for families who are struggling to afford record prices at the pump and in their homes.
stories.other_side: Opponents say that punishing companies for making profits will discourage them from investing in new energy supplies and make future shortages even worse. Others argue that windfall taxes don't actually work well in practice and often just end up being passed on to consumers in the form of even higher prices.

stories.headline: Nifty Weakens as Stocks Approach Critical 21,700 Support Level
stories.summary: India's benchmark Nifty index ended lower after persistent selling drove it below key short-term support levels, with technical indicators remaining bearish as the index approaches the critical 21,700 zone. Market sentiment remains fragile amid global economic uncertainties, with analysts watching the make-or-break level closely.
stories.original_title: Dalal Street Week Ahead: All eyes on 21,700 make-or-break zone as Nifty weakens further — Nifty ended lower after persistent selling dragged the index below key short-term supports, signalling further weakness. With the index approaching the crucial 21,700 zone, sentiment remains fragile amid global uncertainties. Technical indicators stay bearish…
stories.context: The Nifty is India's main stock market index, which is like a scorecard that tracks how the biggest 50 companies in India are performing overall in the stock market. When investors talk about support levels, they mean price points where stocks have historically stopped falling and started bouncing back up - like a floor that the price rarely crashes through. Technical indicators are mathematical tools and patterns that traders study to predict where stock prices might go next, similar to how weather forecasters use data patterns to predict tomorrow's weather. When indicators are "bearish," it means they're showing signals that suggest prices might continue to fall, and when people talk about a "make-or-break level," they mean if the price drops below that point, it could trigger much more selling and cause prices to fall even further.
stories.why_it_matters: India's main stock market is dropping close to a key level that traders watch closely, and if it falls further, it could signal bigger problems for the economy and people's investments. When the stock market stays weak, it can make companies less likely to hire and regular people might lose money in their retirement accounts and savings.
stories.other_side: Some analysts say these are just normal ups and downs in the market and that smart investors shouldn't panic over short-term drops during uncertain times. Others argue that technical analysis is mostly guessing and that regular people should focus on the long-term health of companies rather than watching these short-term market movements.

stories.headline: Dubai Gold Prices Remain Stable Across Major Jewellers
stories.summary: Gold prices in Dubai remained stable across major retailers including Kalyan Jewellers, Joyalukkas, Malabar Gold, and iGold as of April 4, 2026. Current market rates for 24K, 22K, and 18K gold are being quoted against the UAE dirham exchange rate, with Malabar Gold's prices last updated on April 3.
stories.original_title: Gold rates in Dubai today: Latest 24K, 22K prices (April 4, 2026) at Kalyan Jewellers, Joyalukkas, Malabar Gold, iGold — Dubai gold rates: Gold prices in Dubai are stable across major retailers today, April 4, 2026.  Rates for 24K, 22K, and 18K gold are available from Joyalukkas, Kalyan Jewellers, and iGold. Malabar Gold's last update was April 3.  The UAE dirham exchange rate …
stories.context: Gold jewelry is typically measured by purity - 24K gold is 100% pure gold, 22K is about 91.6% gold mixed with other metals to make it stronger, and 18K is 75% gold. Gold is priced globally in US dollars but then converted to local currencies using exchange rates - an exchange rate is simply how much one currency is worth in terms of another currency. The UAE dirham is the official currency of United Arab Emirates (where Dubai is located), and its value is tied to the US dollar at a fixed rate, making gold pricing in Dubai relatively stable compared to places with floating currencies. Different jewelry stores might charge slightly different prices for the same gold because of factors like their brand reputation, store location, how much they paid for the gold when they bought it, and the specific services or craftsmanship they offer.
stories.why_it_matters: Gold prices staying steady in one of the world's biggest gold markets is actually good news for people worried about their money losing value during uncertain times. This stability means that gold is still being seen as a safe place to store wealth when other investments are bouncing around unpredictably.
stories.other_side: Critics point out that gold doesn't earn interest or dividends like other investments, so keeping money in gold means missing out on profits from growing companies. Others argue that gold is old-fashioned technology as a safety measure and there are better modern options for protecting money during uncertain times.

stories.headline: Türkiye Emerges as Strategic Energy Corridor Amid Hormuz Crisis
stories.summary: The severe disruption of shipping through the Strait of Hormuz following the Middle East war has intensified efforts to find alternative energy routes to Europe. Türkiye is increasingly viewed as a critical transit corridor for oil and natural gas flows, highlighting its growing strategic importance in global energy security.
stories.original_title: Hormuz disruption puts Türkiye in focus as alternative energy corridor — Severe disruption in the Strait of Hormuz following the war in the Middle East has intensified the search for alternative energy routes, bringing Türkiye into sharper focus as a possible transit corridor for oil and natural gas flows to Europe.
stories.context: For decades, most oil and natural gas flowing from the Middle East to Europe has traveled through the Strait of Hormuz and across various waterways, making that route essential for European energy security. Energy corridor refers to any path - whether through pipelines, railways, or shipping routes - that allows energy to move from where it's produced to where it's needed. Türkiye sits at the crossroads between Europe, Asia, and the Middle East, making it geographically perfect to serve as an alternative pathway for energy supplies around conflict zones. When one major route (like Hormuz) becomes blocked or dangerous, countries desperately look for backup routes, and this sudden demand makes alternative pathways much more valuable and strategically important than they were before.
stories.why_it_matters: The disruption in the Middle East is making countries look for new ways to get oil and gas, and Türkiye is becoming an important backup route to keep energy flowing to Europe. This is changing which countries have power and influence in the world economy, which could affect where jobs and money flow in the future.
stories.other_side: Critics worry that relying too much on Türkiye for energy shipments gives them too much power over Europe and could be used unfairly during political disagreements. Others argue that building new energy routes costs billions and takes many years, making this more of a long-term plan than an actual solution to current problems.

stories.headline: India Secures Crude Oil Supplies, Dismisses Iranian Import Concerns
stories.summary: India's Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas has assured that crude oil supplies are fully secured for the coming months, firmly rejecting reports of potential disruptions or payment problems with Iranian crude imports. The ministry confirmed that Indian refiners have successfully diversified their sourcing strategies to maintain national energy security.
stories.original_title: India's crude oil supply fully secured, no payment hurdle for Iranian imports: Petroleum Ministry — India's crude oil supplies are fully secured for upcoming months, with the Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas dismissing reports of disruptions and payment issues related to Iranian crude imports. Refiners have diversified sourcing, ensuring energy securit…
stories.context: India is one of the world's largest importers of crude oil, meaning they buy huge amounts of unrefined oil from other countries to power their vehicles, industries, and generate electricity. For a long time, India bought significant oil from Iran, but when other countries imposed sanctions on Iran (basically economic restrictions meant to pressure Iran's government), it became harder to safely pay for Iranian oil. Diversifying sourcing strategies means instead of relying on one or two countries for oil, India started buying from many different countries so that if one supplier has problems, they can still get oil from others. The Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas is India's government department responsible for making sure the country has enough energy, and when they say supplies are "secured," they mean they've lined up enough oil from various sources to keep the country running smoothly for the coming months.
stories.why_it_matters: India telling everyone that it has enough oil secured means the country won't face gas shortages or huge price hikes even as conflict disrupts supplies from Iran. This matters to all Indians because it keeps essential transportation and everyday costs from spiraling out of control during a global crisis.
stories.other_side: Critics say the government might be hiding the real extent of the problem simply to prevent people from panicking about potential fuel shortages. Others argue that even if India has enough secured for now, the country remains vulnerable in the long term and should be focusing more on becoming energy independent rather than depending on unstable foreign suppliers.
